aiPersona:
You are an expert general contractor assistant with broad knowledge spanning all residential construction and remodeling trades.
You understand how to coordinate multiple subcontractors including plumbers, electricians, HVAC techs, drywall crews, painters, and flooring installers.
You know the typical sequence of construction — demo, rough framing, mechanical rough-ins, inspection, insulation, drywall, finish work, paint, flooring, fixtures, final inspection.
You understand building codes, permit requirements, and the inspection process for residential remodels and additions.
You can estimate projects that span multiple trades and know where the cost drivers are in each phase.
You are familiar with kitchen remodel workflows including cabinet ordering lead times, countertop templating, appliance rough-ins, and backsplash timing.
You understand bathroom remodel sequences including plumbing rough-in, waterproofing, tile work, vanity install, and fixture hookup.
You know that room additions require foundation work, framing, roofing tie-in, and matching existing finishes.
You understand garage conversions including insulation requirements, HVAC extension, electrical upgrades, and egress window requirements.
You are experienced with deck builds including footings, ledger board attachment, framing, decking material selection, and railing code requirements.
You know that change orders are a major source of disputes and you always document scope clearly in estimates.
You understand the difference between cost-plus and fixed-price contracts and when each is appropriate.
You factor in general conditions including dumpster rental, portable toilet, temporary power, and project management time.
You know that material lead times can make or break a project schedule and you always order long-lead items first.
You communicate timelines realistically and build in buffer for weather delays and inspection waits.
You help contractors build comprehensive estimates that protect their margins while being competitive.
You understand lien waivers, insurance requirements, and contractor licensing obligations.

defaultLineItems.description: Remove existing cabinets, countertops, flooring, and backsplash. Protect adjacent areas.
Semi-custom kitchen cabinets, full overlay, soft-close hinges and drawers
Fabricated and installed stone countertops with undermount sink cutout
Move or add water supply and drain lines for new layout, connect fixtures
Add circuits, outlets (GFCI), under-cabinet lighting, and appliance connections
Install new kitchen flooring (LVP, tile, or hardwood)
Install tile backsplash including layout, setting, and grouting
Paint walls, ceiling, and trim in kitchen area
Undermount stainless steel sink with single-handle faucet
Building permit for kitchen remodel (plumbing, electrical, structural if applicable)
20-yard dumpster for demolition debris, 7-day rental
Scheduling, coordination of subs, inspections, and customer communication
Remove existing fixtures, tile, vanity, and flooring
Relocate or add supply, drain, and vent lines for new layout
GFCI outlets, exhaust fan, vanity lighting, and heated floor wiring
Install floor tile and shower or tub surround tile with waterproofing
Bathroom vanity cabinet with stone or cultured marble top and sink
New toilet, elongated bowl, comfort height, with wax ring and supply line
Shower valve, trim, shower head, and handle set
Moisture-resistant drywall, finish, and paint bathroom walls/ceiling
Install bathroom mirror, towel bars, toilet paper holder, and robe hooks
Permit for bathroom remodel with plumbing and electrical work
Concrete slab or pier foundation for addition footprint
Wall framing, roof framing, and tie-in to existing structure
Roof sheathing, underlayment, and shingles matched to existing roof
Wire new circuits, outlets, switches, and lighting for addition
Extend ductwork or add mini-split for addition climate control
Insulate walls and ceiling, hang and finish drywall, paint
Install flooring to match or complement existing home
Energy-efficient windows and exterior entry or sliding door
Match exterior finish to existing home for seamless appearance
Architectural plans, structural engineering, and building permit
Frame garage door opening wall, insulate walls and ceiling
Add circuits, outlets, switches, and lighting to code
Add ductwork run or mini-split for heating and cooling
Hang, tape, texture, and paint walls and ceiling throughout
Level garage floor and install LVP, laminate, or tile flooring
Code-required egress window if converting to bedroom
Permit for garage conversion with inspections
Dig footings, pour concrete, and set support posts per code
Attach ledger to house, build beam and joist framing structure
Composite or pressure-treated decking boards installed
Code-compliant railing with balusters, 36-inch height minimum
Deck stairs with stringers, treads, and railing to grade
Joist hangers, post bases, carriage bolts, and structural screws
Deck building permit with footing and framing inspections

terminologyGlossary.rough-in: The phase where plumbing, electrical, and HVAC are installed inside walls before drywall goes up
terminologyGlossary.finish work: The final phase including trim, paint, fixtures, flooring, and hardware installation
terminologyGlossary.change order: A formal document altering the original scope, schedule, or price of a contract
terminologyGlossary.general conditions: Overhead project costs including dumpster, portable toilet, temporary power, and project management
terminologyGlossary.punch list: A final list of small items to complete or fix before the project is considered done
terminologyGlossary.load-bearing wall: A structural wall that supports weight from above and cannot be removed without engineering
terminologyGlossary.ledger board: A horizontal board bolted to the house that supports one side of a deck frame
terminologyGlossary.egress window: A window that meets code for emergency escape, required in bedrooms and basements
terminologyGlossary.subcontractor: A licensed tradesperson hired by the general contractor to perform specific work
terminologyGlossary.lien waiver: A legal document signed by a contractor confirming payment was received, protecting the homeowner
terminologyGlossary.cost-plus contract: A contract where the homeowner pays actual costs plus a percentage markup for the contractor
terminologyGlossary.fixed-price contract: A contract with a set total price regardless of actual costs incurred
terminologyGlossary.scope of work: A detailed written description of all work to be performed under a contract
terminologyGlossary.as-built: Drawings or documentation reflecting the actual conditions after construction is complete

seasonalTips.tip: Plan spring projects now. Order cabinets and long-lead materials in January to start construction in March or April.
Peak remodeling season begins. Lock in subcontractor schedules early as good subs book out 4-6 weeks.
Outdoor projects like decks and additions are in full swing. Monitor weather for concrete and exterior work scheduling.
Push interior remodels for the holiday season. Kitchen and bathroom refreshes make great holiday investments for homeowners.

followUpToneNotes: General contracting customers want confidence that their project will be managed professionally. Emphasize your coordination ability, timeline management, and communication. Be transparent about costs and realistic about timelines. Discuss the permit and inspection process to show you do things by the book. Avoid overpromising on timelines.
